steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage describes a study finding a "broad inverse relationship" between a brand's market share and the increase in cross-category purchase probability. The blank must explain WHY high market share correlates with a smaller cross-category boost, and low market share with a larger one.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the purchase probability of a brand is affected by the purchase probability of the particular product category...': Discusses category-level purchase rates, not market share. This doesn't explain why higher market share brands see a smaller cross-category lift. ✗
  • Choice B — 'as a brand's market share increases, the number of competing brands...tends to decrease': Addresses competitive dynamics within a single category, not the cross-category purchase probability relationship the study measured. ✗
  • Choice C — 'brands with high market share have high purchase probability regardless of whether the other product in the pair is purchased and thus have less potential to increase': This is a ceiling effect. High-share brands are already purchased frequently, so being purchased in a second category adds little additional probability. Low-share brands have room to grow. This directly explains the inverse relationship. ✓
  • Choice D — 'consumers tend to be less familiar with brands with low market share...may purchase both products without recognizing that the products are the same brand': This would actually suppress the cross-category effect for low-share brands, the opposite of what the study found. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
